did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including Vgg19, ResNet101, and InceptionV3, to perform skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dermoscopic image dataset. It employs cosine cyclical learning rates to optimize the models for diagnosing melanoma and other skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study proposing a skin cancer classification model using cosine cyclical learning rates with CNNs (Vgg19, ResNet101, InceptionV3) on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes sections on proposed methods, experimental setup, and results with comparative metrics, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Skin Cancer Classification based on Cosine Cyclical Learning Rate with Deep Learning
journal: 2022 IEEE International Instrumentation and Measurement Technology Conference (I2MTC)
brief_summary: This study proposes a skin cancer classification model utilizing cosine cyclical learning rates to improve melanoma prediction using deep learning. The researchers employed three Convolutional Neural Networks (Vgg19, ResNet101, and InceptionV3) on the HAM10000 dermoscopic image dataset. The models were trained with fixed, cosine annealing, and cosine annealing with warm restarts learning rates, incorporating data augmentation to handle class imbalance. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC metrics across seven skin lesion classes. The results indicated that ResNet101 with warm restarts achieved the highest accuracy of 92%, outperforming the baseline models. The study concludes that cosine cyclical learning rates significantly enhance the diagnostic performance of deep learning models for skin lesions.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc) from the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into seven diagnostic categories. The models (Vgg19, ResNet101, InceptionV3) predict discrete lesion classes. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC, with confusion matrices provided for class-wise prediction results.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of publicly available dermoscopic images dataset containing 10,015 images with duplicates removed (5,514 unique lesion IDs). It includes representative collections of all important diagnostic categories in pigmented lesions.
classes_used: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma / Bowen’s disease (akiec)
Benign keratosis-like lesions (solar lentigines / seborrheic keratoses and lichen-planus like keratoses) (bkl)
Vascular lesions (angiomas, angiokeratomas, pyogenic granulomas and hemorrhage) (vasc)

use.training: Used for training the CNN models (Vgg19, ResNet101, InceptionV3) with data augmentation to handle class imbalance.
use.validation: 5,514 images without repeated lesion IDs were classified as the validation set.
use.testing: The paper states the model was only tested on its own divided dataset (training/validation split) and did not test on an external unified test set like ISIC 2018 because ground truth was not public.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide detailed information about correct and incorrect skin cancer classifications and to calculate precision, recall, and F1-score for each class.
To average evaluation indicators (precision, recall, F1-score) across different classes, giving all classes the same weight.
To average evaluation indicators across different classes, weighting them according to the true distribution ratio of the classes to handle class imbalance.
To divide the validation set according to lesion type labels to ensure representative distribution.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.split: Training (4322 images after augmentation) and Validation (1655 images stratified by lesion type)
HAM10000.experiment: Comparison of three CNN architectures (Vgg19, ResNet101, InceptionV3) with three learning rate strategies (Fixed/Baseline, Cosine Annealing, Cosine Annealing with Warm Restarts)
HAM10000.notes: ResNet101 with warm restarts achieved the highest overall accuracy (92%), precision, F1-score, and AUC. The paper notes that macro-average metrics can be misleading in imbalanced datasets like HAM10000, and weighted-average metrics provide a more representative evaluation. The models were not tested on an external unified test set (e.g., ISIC 2018) because ground truth was not public.

architecture_used.main_model: Three Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): Vgg19, ResNet101, and InceptionV3
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models (standard deep feature extraction modules)

architecture_details.Vgg19: Improvement of VGGNet over AlexNet using several consecutive 3x3 convolution kernels to replace larger kernels (11x11, 5x5) for a given receptive field. Uses stacked small convolution kernels to increase network depth with fewer parameters.
architecture_details.ResNet101: Uses residual modules to train a deeper network, allowing gradients to pass smoothly without exploding or disappearing. Chosen as one of the classifiers.
architecture_details.InceptionV3: Evolution of GoogleNet where 7x7 kernels are split into 1x7 and 7x1 (Factorization into small convolutions). Reduces top-5 error rate to 3.5% on ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC) 2012. Has a total of 42 layers.

learning_rates: Cosine Annealing LR: Initial 0.001, changed periodically five times per epoch
Cosine Annealing with Warm Restarts: Initial 0.001, restarts after four epochs
training_details.hardware: Linux system with GPU acceleration (Nvidia GeForce RTX 2080Ti)
data_augmentation: Class-specific expansion for unbalanced classes (except nv)

class_labels: akiec (Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma / Bowen’s disease)

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: random vertical and horizontal flipping
Data augmentation: adjustments of color distributions
Class-specific expansion (oversampling) for unbalanced classes to handle class imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to unbalanced classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, vasc) by expanding them by corresponding multiples to match the count of the majority class (nv). Specific final training counts: Melanoma 6264, Basal cell carcinoma 5532, Vascular lesions 5412, Actinic keratoses 5358, Dermatofibroma 5356, Benign keratosis-like lesions 4835. The nv class was not expanded (5380).
Geometric transform applied to standardize input dimensions.

Color transform applied to increase dataset variability.

conclusions: The proposed method using cosine cyclical learning rates (specifically Cosine Annealing with Warm Restarts) significantly improves the diagnostic performance of deep learning models for skin lesions compared to baseline fixed learning rates.
ResNet101 with warm restarts achieved the highest overall performance, reaching an accuracy of 92%, along with the highest precision, F1-score, and AUC among all tested models.
InceptionV3 with warm restarts also achieved a high accuracy of 92%, performing comparably to ResNet101.
Vgg19 showed improvements with cosine cyclical learning rates, with accuracy increasing by 3% with cosine annealing and 5% with warm restarts compared to its baseline.
Using macro-average metrics is inappropriate for evaluating models on imbalanced datasets like HAM10000; weighted-average metrics provide a more representative evaluation of performance.

The study acknowledges insufficient model generalization because it was only tested on its own divided dataset (training/validation split) and not on an external unified test set like ISIC 2018 due to the lack of public ground truth.

The authors conclude that skin lesion diagnosis based on deep learning offers potential for earlier detection and treatment, which could improve survival rates, and suggest future work should focus on testing more deep networks.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; the authors explicitly state that the model was not tested on an external unified test set (e.g., ISIC 2018) because the ground truth was not public, limiting the assessment of generalization capability.

The HAM10000 dataset is highly imbalanced (e.g., nv: 5380 vs. df: 103 raw images); the paper acknowledges that macro-average metrics are misleading for such datasets and recommends using weighted-average metrics for more representative evaluation.

Data augmentation involved class-specific expansion (oversampling) for unbalanced classes to match the majority class count, which may introduce bias or overfitting risks for minority classes like dermatofibroma (df) and vascular lesions (vasc).

Performance on the melanoma (mel) class remains relatively low compared to benign nevi (nv), with precision ranging from 0.27 to 0.61 across models, indicating potential difficulty in distinguishing melanoma from other classes despite overall high accuracy.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between models or learning rate strategies.

The validation set was derived from the same source as the training data (HAM10000) with stratified splitting, rather than using a separate, independent clinical cohort, which raises concerns about data leakage or over-optimistic performance estimates in a real-world clinical setting.
